clean_story: Tiny invisible attractions.  Holding things together—without glue. "Van der Waals forces" are named after Dutch scientist Johannes Diderik van der Waals, who studied how gases turn into liquids—and realized even neutral atoms could pull on each other like weak magnets. Imagine two sheets of paper sticking slightly when pressed together. No tape. No static. Just a faint, temporary grip from the atoms themselves. Here’s the trick:Electrons in every atom are always moving—sometimes clustering on one side, creating a lopsided charge for a fraction of a second. Nearby atoms respond, their electrons shifting to "mirror" the imbalance. Result?A fleeting pull—like two dancers briefly syncing steps before drifting apart. This is why geckos walk on ceilings. Their foot pads are covered in microscopic hairs, each splitting into tinier bristles that get close enough to surfaces for van der Waals forces to activate—millions of tiny handshakes holding their weight. It’s also why graphite (pencil lead) feels slippery. Layers of carbon atoms slide past each other, held just loosely enough by these forces to smear onto paper. Three related subjects are molecular bonds, surface tension, and gecko adhesion.
